# stream-fork — full reference

`stream-fork` is a 1→N stream combinator. It exports a single class, `Fork`, which extends Node's `Writable` and duplicates every chunk to N downstream Writables while propagating backpressure from the slowest downstream.

This file is the long-form LLM-consumable reference. For the project's architectural overview see [ARCHITECTURE.md](./ARCHITECTURE.md); for AI-agent rules see [AGENTS.md](./AGENTS.md); for an end-user-facing summary see [llms.txt](./llms.txt) and the [README](./README.md).

## Why this package exists

Node's `Writable.write(chunk, encoding, callback)` invokes `callback` when the chunk has been consumed (either flushed downstream or accepted into a buffer with room to spare). The upstream pipe checks the return value of `write` (false = high-water-mark exceeded → pause) and listens for `'drain'` to resume.

Node has no built-in primitive for backpressure-preserving fan-out:

- `Readable.pipe(dest)` is 1:1.
- Piping a single Readable into multiple destinations sets up multiple readers off the same source; the source slows to the slowest, but there is no slot for per-destination completion gating in a Writable context.
- Calling `outputs.forEach(o => o.write(chunk))` and ignoring the callbacks discards backpressure entirely; the slow downstream's internal buffer grows unboundedly.

`stream-fork` fills that gap. The Fork is itself a Writable, so it composes anywhere a Writable is expected (e.g. `source.pipe(fork)` or as a leaf in a `stream-chain` pipeline). Its `_write` runs `Promise.all` over each downstream's `write` callback, so its own callback only fires once every downstream has acknowledged consumption.

## Installation

```bash
npm i stream-fork
```

Node 22+ required.

## Public API

### `Fork`

Default export. A Writable subclass.

```js
const Fork = require('stream-fork');

const fork = new Fork(outputs, options);
```

#### Constructor: `new Fork(outputs[, options])`

- `outputs` (required) — array of downstream Writables. Each entry must be a Writable (or a Transform/Duplex used as a Writable). The array may be empty (`isEmpty()` will report `true`).
- `options` (optional) — passed through to the `Writable` super-constructor. Default: `{objectMode: true}`. Opt out of object mode by passing an empty object `{}` for chunk mode (Buffer/string).
  - `options.ignoreErrors` (optional boolean) — controls error propagation. See § Error handling below.

The constructor wires error listeners on every downstream:

- A no-op `'error'` listener (`stream.on('error', () => {})`) on every downstream. This prevents Node's default uncaught-error handler from crashing the process if a downstream errors before any of Fork's own logic runs.
- When `!ignoreErrors`, an additional listener filters the failed stream out of `outputs` and re-emits the error on the Fork (only if writing hasn't started — once `_write` has been called once, `startedWriting === true` and post-start errors propagate via the per-write Promise instead).

#### `Fork.fork(outputs[, options])`

Static factory. Equivalent to `new Fork(outputs, options)`. Lets you write `dataSource.pipe(fork([s1, s2]))` without `new`.

```js
const {fork} = require('stream-fork');

source.pipe(fork([s1, s2]));
```

#### `fork.outputs`

The downstream Writables array passed to the constructor, mutated in place. When a downstream errors out, it is set to `null` for the duration of the current write round, then filtered out by `processResults`. After the filtering, `outputs` contains only the still-healthy downstreams.

#### `fork.isEmpty()`

Returns `true` if `outputs.length === 0`. Useful for detecting that every downstream has errored out and the Fork is effectively a sink that discards data.

## Backpressure mechanics

The Fork's `_write` implementation:

```js
_write(chunk, encoding, callback) {
  this.startedWriting = true;
  Promise.all(this.outputs.map(waitForWrite(chunk, encoding))).then(this.processResults(callback));
}
```

Where `waitForWrite(chunk, encoding)(output)` returns a Promise that:

1. Calls `output.write(chunk, encoding, cb)`.
2. Resolves when `cb` fires (with the error, or `null`).

Because `_write`'s own `callback` is only called once `Promise.all` resolves, the slowest downstream gates the upstream:

- If 3 of 4 downstreams ack immediately but the 4th sits on its buffer, the Promise.all hangs on the 4th.
- The Fork's `_write` callback hangs until the 4th acks.
- The upstream pipe sees the deferred ack as backpressure and pauses the source.
- When the slow downstream drains, its callback fires, Promise.all resolves, Fork's callback runs, upstream resumes.

This is the correct shape: each downstream's callback is what gates the upstream.

`_final(callback)` works the same way but with `output.end(cb)` instead of `output.write(chunk, encoding, cb)`. All downstreams are closed in parallel and the Fork waits for each one to acknowledge.

## Error handling

Two modes, controlled by `options.ignoreErrors`.

### Default mode (`!ignoreErrors`)

Errors can arrive through two channels:

1. **Downstream emits `'error'`.** The per-stream listener removes the stream from `outputs` and — if writing hasn't started yet (`!startedWriting`) — re-emits `'error'` on the Fork. Once writing has started, post-start `'error'` events from downstreams are still seen by the listener (which filters the stream from `outputs`), but the Fork itself doesn't re-emit because the error will surface through the write-callback path.
2. **Downstream's write-callback reports an error.** `waitForWrite`'s inner callback sees the error, nulls the slot (`array[index] = null`), and resolves the per-output Promise with the error. After `Promise.all`, `reportErrors` iterates the resolved values, calls `callback(error)` with the first error encountered (firing `'error'` on the Fork via the Writable contract), and filters nulls out of `outputs`.

Either way, the failing downstream is removed and subsequent writes skip it. `'error'` fires **once** on the Fork per write round (first error wins).

### Ignore mode (`ignoreErrors: true`)

The per-stream re-emit listener is not installed. The no-op listener is still installed (so Node doesn't crash on unhandled errors). When a downstream's write-callback reports an error, the slot is still nulled, but `ignoreErrors` calls `callback(null)` unconditionally — the error is swallowed. The failing downstream is filtered out of `outputs` and subsequent writes skip it.

`'error'` never fires on the Fork in this mode.

## Examples

### Fan out to multiple sinks

```js
const Fork = require('stream-fork');
const fs = require('node:fs');
const zlib = require('node:zlib');

const gzip = zlib.createGzip();
gzip.pipe(fs.createWriteStream('log.txt.gz'));

const forkStream = new Fork([gzip, process.stdout], {});
dataSource.pipe(forkStream);
```

### Tolerate flaky downstreams

```js
const Fork = require('stream-fork');

source.pipe(
  new Fork([primarySink, flakyMetricsSink], {ignoreErrors: true})
);
```

When `flakyMetricsSink` errors, the failing downstream is silently filtered out. `primarySink` continues receiving data, and the source pipe is unaffected.

### Per-output transforms (composition)

`stream-fork` doesn't bake per-output transforms in; compose them externally:

```js
const Fork = require('stream-fork');
const chain = require('stream-chain');

source.pipe(
  new Fork([
    chain([compress, fileSink]),
    chain([stringify, networkSink])
  ])
);
```

### Detecting "all downstreams failed"

```js
const Fork = require('stream-fork');

const fork = new Fork([sinkA, sinkB], {ignoreErrors: true});
source.pipe(fork);

fork.on('finish', () => {
  if (fork.isEmpty()) {
    console.error('every downstream errored out');
  }
});
```

## Object mode vs chunk mode

The default options literal is `{objectMode: true}`. To use Fork in chunk mode (Buffer/string), pass an explicit empty options object:

```js
new Fork([sinkA, sinkB]);       // object mode (default)
new Fork([sinkA, sinkB], {});   // chunk mode (Buffer/string)
```

Or pass an explicit `objectMode` value:

```js
new Fork([sinkA, sinkB], {objectMode: false});  // chunk mode
new Fork([sinkA, sinkB], {objectMode: true});   // object mode
```

The chosen mode is propagated to the Fork's own Writable contract via the super-constructor; the downstream streams' modes must be compatible.

## Notable changes in 2.x

- Node 22+ required.
- Repackaged under `src/index.js` with hand-written `.d.ts` sidecar; the old `main.js` entry point is removed.
- Test runner moved from `heya-unit` to `tape-six`.
- The algorithm and public class shape are unchanged from 1.x.

## Links

- Repository: https://github.com/uhop/stream-fork
- npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/stream-fork
- AGENTS.md: agent-facing rules
- ARCHITECTURE.md: design overview
